issues.title: WSL 2 mirrored networking breaks NFS mounts ("Connection timed out")
issues.next_action_reason: Long-standing high-reach networking bug with community-verified root cause + workaround that the team has never acknowledged; ack the workaround, add labels, and prioritize a real fix.
issues.suggested_reply:
Thanks everyone for the detailed investigation here. Summarizing the community findings for the team and other users:

- Root cause appears to be that in mirrored networking mode, NFS mounts using privileged (reserved, <1024) source ports fail to complete the TCP handshake (SYN+ACK not delivered to the WSL socket).
- Confirmed workaround: mount with the `noresvport` option, e.g. `sudo mount -t nfs -o noresvport <HOST>:/share /mnt/share` (also works via `/etc/auto.net` / autofs and docker-compose).

We'll add `workaround-available` and keep this tracked for a proper fix. Could someone still hitting this on the latest 2.7.x confirm whether `noresvport` resolves it for them so we can prioritize?

issues.title: [ARM64 / Qualcomm Adreno 680] GPU acceleration unavailable in WSL2 — /dev/dri/renderD128 not created by dxgkrnl, Mesa falls back to llvmpipe
issues.next_action_reason: Likely gated on host WDDM/GPU-PV driver support, not a WSLg defect — confirm the Windows Adreno WDDM version before routing to Qualcomm/Windows graphics or closing as not-supported.
issues.suggested_reply: Thanks @dorepi — the diagnosis is thorough. The key detail is that `/dev/dxg` exists but `/dev/dri/renderD128` is never created: that node is created by `dxgkrnl` only when the host GPU exposes a WDDM driver with GPU paravirtualization (GPU-PV / WDDMv2.9+) support. As @Gerdya notes, several Qualcomm Adreno drivers (680 / X1) historically don't advertise the GPU-PV capability that WSL GPU compute/D3D12 requires, which is why Mesa falls back to llvmpipe regardless of the Linux-side config. Two things that would confirm this: (1) in Windows, does `dxdiag` show a WDDM 2.9+ driver for the Adreno, and (2) does `wsl --status` / `Get-WmiObject Win32_VideoController` report GPU-PV support? If the host driver doesn't expose GPU-PV this isn't fixable from the WSLg side and would need a Qualcomm/Windows graphics-driver update — but let's confirm the WDDM version first before we route/close.
issues.owner_evidence: GPU-PV / dxgkrnl device creation depends on the Windows-side Qualcomm graphics driver (WDDM GPU virtualization), outside WSLg; no CODEOWNERS to name an owner.
issues.owner_fallback_reason: GPU-PV / dxgkrnl device creation depends on the Windows-side Qualcomm graphics driver (WDDM GPU virtualization), outside WSLg; no CODEOWNERS to name an owner.

issues.title: WSLg 1.0.71: GUI session freezes, then Weston becomes unkillable in /mnt/shared_memory virtiofs
issues.next_action_reason: Brand-new, top-severity virtiofs hang with kernel stacks and a diagnostics zip; acknowledge quickly, cross-link the related shared-memory regression #1456, and ask the two scoping questions before it goes cold.
issues.suggested_reply: Thanks @Mygod — this is an unusually thorough report and the kernel stacks are exactly what we need. To summarize for tracking: WSLg GUI surfaces freeze together while the rest of the distro stays responsive; Weston (PID 14) then wedges in uninterruptible D-state (`wchan: request_wait_answer`) blocked in a FUSE `openat()` on `/mnt/shared_memory/{guid}` (the `wslg` virtiofs / virtio1), and unbinding virtio1 also hangs in `virtio_fs_drain_queue` — so a shared-memory virtiofs request appears to be stuck, which is why restarting msrdc.exe/apps can't recover it. This may be related to the shared-memory virtiofs regressions we're seeing in #1456. Two questions: (1) does it correlate with the host RDP client (msrdc.exe) dropping/reconnecting just before the freeze? (2) can you reproduce after a fresh `wsl --shutdown`, or only on long-lived sessions? We'll dig into the virtiofs request/queue path.
